name: ipcraft-vscode
description: Visual editor for IP Core (IPCraft) and Memory Map specifications

commands.title: Vivado Not Found — Click to Configure
Quartus Not Found — Click to Configure

No Build Tools Configured — Click to Configure

editor/title.when: resourceExtname == .vhd && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceExtname == .vhdl && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceExtname == .v && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceExtname == .sv && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview'

commandPalette.when: resourceExtname == .vhd || resourceExtname == .vhdl || resourceExtname == .v || resourceExtname == .sv || resourceFilename =~ /_hw\.tcl$/ || resourceFilename == component.xml

ipcraft.busLibraryPaths.markdownDescription: Paths to search recursively for custom bus definition YAML files (`.yml` / `.yaml`). Absolute paths or paths relative to the workspace root. Files ending in `.ip.yml` or `.mm.yml` are ignored.

ipcraft.ipRepositoryPaths.markdownDescription: Additional file system paths to scan for IP cores (directories containing `.ip.yml` or `component.xml` files).

ipcraft.gui.display.markdownDescription: DISPLAY environment variable to use for GUI tools (Vivado, Quartus, Platform Designer). Useful for Remote SSH / X11 forwarding (e.g. `localhost:10.0` or `:100` for xpra).

ipcraft.gui.xauthority.markdownDescription: XAUTHORITY environment variable to use for GUI tools. Needed for X11 forwarding in some Remote SSH setups.

ipcraft.scaffoldPackPaths.markdownDescription: Additional file system paths to scan recursively for custom Scaffold Packs (directories containing `scaffold.yml` files).

ipcraft.generate.targets.markdownDescription: Synthesis vendor targets to generate packaging files for. Each entry must match a registered toolchain id (e.g. `vivado`, `quartus`). An empty array generates HDL and testbench only.

ipcraft.generate.includeTestbench.description: Generate a cocotb testbench (tb/ folder) when scaffolding a project.

ipcraft.generate.hdlLanguage.description: HDL language for RTL file generation (VHDL or SystemVerilog).

ipcraft.generate.scaffoldPack.markdownDescription: Scaffold pack to use for RTL and testbench code generation. Built-in values: `builtin-minimal`, `builtin-ipcraft`. Example packs: `example-register-only`, `example-no-regfile`, `example-with-docs`. Workspace packs live in `.vscode/ipcraft/packs/<name>/`.

enumDescriptions: Generate a CocoTB testbench (Makefile + Python test)
Generate a VUnit testbench (run.py + VHDL testbench entity)
ipcraft.testbench.framework.description: Testbench framework used by 'Generate CocoTB Testbench'.

ipcraft.testbench.engine.description: Simulation engine used by the generated testbench.

ipcraft.toolbar.targets.markdownDescription: Which vendor toolchain sections to display in the IP Core editor toolbar. Each entry must match a registered toolchain id (e.g. `vivado`, `quartus`).

ipcraft.import.vendor.description: Vendor name used when importing IP cores (VHDL, hw.tcl, component.xml). Leave blank or 'user' to auto-detect from git user.email (e.g. user@company.com → company.com), falling back to 'ipcraft'.
ipcraft.import.vendor.markdownDescription: Vendor name used when importing IP cores (VHDL, `_hw.tcl`, `component.xml`). Leave blank or `user` to auto-detect from git `user.email` (e.g. `user@company.com` → `company.com`), falling back to `ipcraft`.

ipcraft.import.library.description: Default library name used when importing a VHDL file as an IP Core.

ipcraft.import.version.description: Default version string used when importing a VHDL file as an IP Core.

enumDescriptions: Use a locally installed Vivado. Configure ipcraft.vivado.installDir.
Run Vivado inside a Docker container. Configure ipcraft.vivado.dockerImage.
ipcraft.vivado.runner.description: How IPCraft executes Vivado commands. 'local' uses a native install; 'docker' runs every command inside a container.

ipcraft.vivado.installDir.markdownDescription:
_(Used when `runner` is `local`)_ Path to your Vivado installation directory. IPCraft will locate the correct executable automatically.

**Linux:** `/tools/Xilinx/Vivado/2024.2`

**Windows:** `C:\Xilinx\2025.1\Vivado`

Leave empty to use `vivado` from your system PATH.

ipcraft.vivado.dockerImage.markdownDescription: _(Used when `runner` is `docker`)_ Docker image used to run Vivado (e.g. `cvsoc/vivado:2024.2`). Every Vivado command is executed inside this container via `docker run --rm`, with the IP core directory mounted as `/work`.

ipcraft.vivado.defaultPart.markdownDescription: Fallback part number used when no board has been selected yet. The board picker remembers the last-used selection across sessions, so this setting rarely needs to be changed manually.

ipcraft.customBoards.vivado.markdownDescription: Your own boards for Vivado. Each entry appears at the top of the board picker under **My Boards**.

label.description: Display name shown in the board picker (e.g. My Zynq Module)

ipcraft.vivado.pinnedPart.markdownDescription: Pinned Vivado target part number. When set, the board picker is skipped and this value is used directly. Leave empty to always be prompted. Supports **User** and **Workspace** scope so each project can target a different device.

enumDescriptions: Use a locally installed Quartus. Configure ipcraft.quartus.installDir.
Run Quartus inside a Docker container. Configure ipcraft.quartus.dockerImage.
ipcraft.quartus.runner.description: How IPCraft executes Quartus commands. 'local' uses a native install; 'docker' runs every command inside a container.

ipcraft.quartus.installDir.markdownDescription: _(Used when `runner` is `local`)_ Top-level Quartus installation directory (e.g. `/opt/intelFPGA_pro/23.1` on Linux or `C:\intelFPGA_pro\23.1` on Windows). When set, IPCraft automatically locates `quartus_sh`, `quartus`, and `qsys-edit` inside the installation, searching `quartus/bin` (Linux) or `quartus/bin64` (Windows) first. Supersedes the individual `shellPath`, `guiPath`, and `qsysEditPath` settings.

ipcraft.quartus.dockerImage.markdownDescription: _(Used when `runner` is `docker`)_ Docker image used to run Quartus (e.g. `cvsoc/quartus:23.1`). Every Quartus command is executed inside this container via `docker run --rm`, with the IP core directory mounted as `/work`.

ipcraft.quartus.defaultDevice.markdownDescription: Fallback device number used when no board has been selected yet. The board picker remembers the last-used selection across sessions, so this setting rarely needs to be changed manually.

ipcraft.customBoards.quartus.markdownDescription: Your own boards for Quartus. Each entry appears at the top of the board picker under **My Boards**.

label.description: Display name shown in the board picker (e.g. My Cyclone V Board)

ipcraft.quartus.pinnedDevice.markdownDescription: Pinned Quartus target device. When set, the board picker is skipped and this value is used directly. Leave empty to always be prompted. Supports **User** and **Workspace** scope so each project can target a different device.

ipcraft.build.jobs.description: Number of parallel jobs passed to Vivado launch_runs and Quartus compilation.

keybindings.when: !terminalFocus && (resourceExtname == .vhd && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceExtname == .vhdl && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceExtname == .v && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceExtname == .sv && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceFilename =~ /_hw\.tcl$/ && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview' || resourceFilename == component.xml && activeCustomEditorId != 'fpgaIpCore.sourcePreview')

walkthroughs.title: Get Started with Scaffold Packs
walkthroughs.description: Learn how to customise what IPCraft generates for your IP cores — file layout, naming conventions, and non-HDL artifacts.

steps.title: Pick your starting point
steps.description:
Export a built-in pack to your workspace. IPCraft includes five packs — two production-ready and three annotated examples.

[Export a Scaffold Pack](command:fpga-ip-core.exportScaffoldPack)

steps.title: Open scaffold.yml
steps.description: The manifest lists every file the pack generates. The Scaffold Pack Preview panel updates as you edit it.

steps.title: Preview a template side by side
steps.description:
Open any .j2 file and click the preview icon to see rendered output against a real IP core context.

[Preview Template Output](command:fpga-ip-core.previewTemplateOutput)

steps.title: Customise the templates
steps.description: Edit .j2 files to add company headers, change coding style, or generate entirely new file types.

steps.title: Wire the pack to an IP core
steps.description:
Add scaffold_pack: to .ip.yml, then run Generate HDL to see your customised output.

[Generate HDL](command:fpga-ip-core.generateHdl)

walkthroughs.title: Design Your First IP Core
walkthroughs.description: Go from an empty folder to generated RTL — canvas, bus interfaces, ports, and your first scaffold.

steps.title: Create your IP core file
steps.description:
An IP core lives in a .ip.yml file. Create one now — IPCraft will open it in the visual canvas.

[New IP Core](command:fpga-ip-core.createIpCore)

steps.title: Meet the canvas
steps.description: The canvas shows your IP core as a block diagram. Clocks and resets on the left, ports and bus interfaces on the right. Click any element to inspect and edit it in the Inspector panel.

steps.title: Connect a bus interface
steps.description:
Drag a bus type from the Library Palette onto the canvas, or explore the built-in bus definitions to see what is available.

[View Bus Definitions](command:fpga-ip-core.viewBusDefinitions)

steps.title: Add clocks, resets, and ports
steps.description: Use the Clocks, Resets, and Ports sections in the left navigation to define your IP core's external signals. Everything you add appears on the canvas instantly.

steps.title: Generate your RTL
steps.description:
Scaffold Full Project generates VHDL or SystemVerilog, a testbench, and vendor packaging files — all in one command.

[Scaffold Full Project](command:fpga-ip-core.scaffoldProject)

steps.title: Review and accept generated files
steps.description: The staging overlay lists every file that will be written. Green dot = new, orange dot = modified. Click View Diff before accepting to inspect any change.

walkthroughs.title: IP Core with a Register Map
walkthroughs.description: Create a memory-mapped IP core with AXI-Lite or Avalon-MM registers — IPCraft generates the bus wrapper and register decode automatically.

steps.title: Create a linked IP core and register map
steps.description:
This command creates both an .ip.yml and an .mm.yml, linked together from the start.

[New IP Core + Register Map](command:fpga-ip-core.createIpCoreWithMemoryMap)

steps.title: Define your control registers
steps.description: The memory map editor opens as a table. Add address blocks, registers, and bit fields. The bit-field visualizer lets you drag and resize fields visually.

steps.title: Generate RTL with register decode
steps.description:
IPCraft auto-wires the bus slave interface from your register map and generates a complete register file decoder.

[Scaffold Full Project](command:fpga-ip-core.scaffoldProject)

steps.title: Inspect the generated register file
steps.description: Open the generated *_regfile.vhd (or *_regfile.sv) to see your register constants, field offsets, and the read/write decoder logic.

steps.title: Verify with a cocotb test
steps.description: A cocotb test skeleton and Makefile were generated alongside your RTL. Run `make SIM=ghdl` to simulate your register interface immediately.

walkthroughs.title: Bring Your VHDL into IPCraft
walkthroughs.description: Parse an existing .vhd entity into a visual IP core spec — IPCraft auto-detects clocks, resets, and bus interfaces.

steps.title: Import your VHDL entity
steps.description:
Point IPCraft at a .vhd file. The parser reads your entity ports and produces a .ip.yml automatically.

[Parse VHDL to .ip.yml](command:fpga-ip-core.parseVHDL)

steps.title: Review what IPCraft detected
steps.description: The canvas shows every port, clock, reset, and bus interface the parser found. Click any element to inspect and correct its properties.

steps.title: Fill in what auto-detection missed
steps.description: Drag a bus interface from the Library Palette if it was not detected, or use the Bus Interfaces tab to add it manually. Set clock frequencies for timing-aware packaging.

steps.title: Package for your target tool
steps.description:
Generate a Vivado component.xml for the IP Catalog, or a Platform Designer _hw.tcl — both from the same .ip.yml spec.

[Export Xilinx component.xml](command:fpga-ip-core.exportXilinx)

[Export Platform Designer _hw.tcl](command:fpga-ip-core.exportAltera)

steps.title: Copy a component instantiation
steps.description:
IPCraft generates a ready-to-paste VHDL or SystemVerilog instantiation — copy it and drop it straight into your top-level design.

[Copy Component Instance](command:fpga-ip-core.copyComponentInstance)

walkthroughs.title: Import from Xilinx or Intel Tools
walkthroughs.description: Convert a Platform Designer _hw.tcl or Vivado component.xml into a portable .ip.yml — then generate for the other vendor.

steps.title: Preview the source file
steps.description:
Open your _hw.tcl or component.xml and press Ctrl+Shift+V to see a read-only canvas — verify the block diagram before converting.

[Preview in IPCraft](command:fpga-ip-core.previewInIpcraft)

steps.title: Convert to a portable spec
steps.description:
Parse the vendor file into a .ip.yml that works with any toolchain.

[Parse Platform Designer _hw.tcl](command:fpga-ip-core.parseHwTcl)

[Parse Xilinx component.xml](command:fpga-ip-core.parseComponentXml)

steps.title: Verify bus interfaces and parameters
steps.description: Check that bus interface port maps and parameter types came through correctly. The Inspector panel lets you edit any property inline.

steps.title: Generate for the other platform
steps.description:
The same .ip.yml can generate both Xilinx and Intel packaging. Cross-vendor conversion is one command.

[Export Xilinx component.xml](command:fpga-ip-core.exportXilinx)

[Export Platform Designer _hw.tcl](command:fpga-ip-core.exportAltera)

steps.title: Open in the native tool for final validation
steps.description:
Launch Vivado IP Packager or Quartus Platform Designer directly from VS Code to validate the generated descriptor.

[Edit in Vivado IP Packager](command:fpga-ip-core.editInIpPackager)

[Open in Platform Designer](command:fpga-ip-core.editInPlatformDesigner)

walkthroughs.title: Synthesize and Check Timing
walkthroughs.description: Run Out-of-Context synthesis in Vivado or Quartus from VS Code and read WNS, utilization, and CDC violations in the Build Reports panel.

steps.title: Point IPCraft at your toolchain
steps.description:
Set the path to Vivado or Quartus in extension settings. A Docker runner is available if you do not have tools installed locally.

[Open IPCraft Settings](command:fpga-ip-core.openSettings)

steps.title: Run Out-of-Context synthesis
steps.description:
OOC synthesis is roughly 5x faster than full implementation — ideal for verifying your IP core's timing and resource usage early.

[Generate & Build (Vivado OOC)](command:fpga-ip-core.generateAndBuildVivado)

[Generate & Build (Quartus)](command:fpga-ip-core.generateAndBuildQuartus)

steps.title: Read timing and utilization in VS Code
steps.description:
The Build Reports panel shows WNS/WHS (Vivado) or Fmax (Quartus), LUT/FF/BRAM/DSP usage, and CDC violations — click any metric to open the raw report.

[Show Build Output](command:fpga-ip-core.showBuildOutput)

steps.title: Run full synthesis and implementation
steps.description:
Once OOC timing looks clean, run a full compile to get final placed-and-routed results.

[Build](command:fpga-ip-core.build)

scripts.pretest: node scripts/check-submodule.js && npm run compile-tests && npm run compile && npm run lint
scripts.test:all: npm run test:unit && npm run test:e2e && npm run test:browser
